headline: Civil servant WFH: fuel saver, workplace reform, or both?
context: Phased flexible/WFH for federal public servants in specified locations was fine-tuned in the 26 March 2026 PMO speech. A PSD circular followed (effective 15 April) with an 8 km commute threshold, SPOT-Me geolocation attendance and KPI/output requirements. Frontline services are excluded.

cards.big: WFH for federal public servants was fine-tuned on 26 March, the day fuel quotas were also cut.
cards.sub: The stated trigger is fuel conservation amid the Hormuz crisis. A PSD circular was issued by 2 April; implementation started 15 April.

cards.big: Frontline workers are excluded by design: nurses, teachers, police. Eligibility is for federal public servants in specified locations with an 8 km commute threshold.
cards.sub: Top management (JUSA C and above) was 60.7% male in 2024; Grade 41-54 was 67.9% female. The distributional impact depends on which slice is in scope.

cards.big: Budget 2026 estimates federal emoluments at RM109.4B and retirement charges at RM42.8B. The public-service headcount is large by international comparisons.
cards.sub: World Bank cautions that cross-country civil-service ratios are difficult to compare. Wage-bill stress is the firmer claim.

cards.big: The UK runs a hybrid model with attendance targets. Singapore mandates flexible-work request rights. Malaysia's 2 April PSD circular sets KPIs and SPOT-Me geolocation attendance.
cards.sub: Penang has declined to implement state-level WFH for now; Sarawak has not issued a state directive. State alignment is uneven.

cards.big: The question is no longer whether WFH was 'announced with no details'. It is whether the published rules will be enforced and whether output, not attendance, becomes the measure.
Crisis-born reforms can stick if the rules outlast the crisis. The PSD circular is a real document; the test is whether departments measure output and publish results.
